stories.title: The Glassblower's Covenant
stories.text:
Kael the glassblower's hands trembled as she shaped the molten breath into a vial. Three winters she'd labored in the Hearthlands' Furnace Quarter, crafting ordinary vessels for merchants. Then the fae-touched woman arrived with a proposition: blow glass that holds starlight, and she would love her. Not force—*would*. A promise burning hotter than any forge.

Kael had heard the tales. Fae bargains devour the soul. But she'd never been loved, never been *seen*. So she worked. Glass that sang when light passed through it. Glass that warmed to the touch of honest hands. Glass that showed reflections of roads not taken.

The woman—Maelis—returned each dawn, drawn by the artistry like a moth to flame. They shared silences in the glow of the furnace. On the tenth night, Maelis's hand found Kael's. Real. Warm. Binding.

"The covenant is sealed," Kael whispered.

Maelis smiled, sad and fey. "Now the price begins. One year more—then I must return to the Twilight Court. The starlight I gave you will fade. You'll remember love but never feel it again."

Kael's hands stilled. She looked at the vials lined along the shelf, each one glowing like a tiny star. "I'll have had one perfect year."

"Yes," Maelis said. "And I'll carry it across the boundaries forever."

They returned to the furnace together, melting glass, burning bright, knowing the ending—and choosing the flame anyway.

stories.title: The Refugee's Second Name
stories.text: At the Pale Crossing waystation, a traveler discovers that her name—the one she's carried since fleeing the Burned Expanse—no longer matches her in the ledgers. The Counter-Archive keeps two records of her: one dead, one alive. She must choose which testimony to keep, knowing that both are true and neither is safe. Around her, water rations grow thinner, and merchants whisper that the nomad routes are shifting. The Wanderer appears at dusk, silent, asking only: "Which version of you survives?" By dawn, she understands the question was never hers to answer.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Refusal
stories.text: Moriel, apprentice bone-singer of the Sunken Cathedrals, begins carving the Elder Tongue into bone—the same work Kess taught her. But the dead have stopped singing back. The pre-human voices that once guided her hands have withdrawn into silence, and the transformed merchants waiting for final transcendence grow restless, hungry, watching. She realizes the bone-songs are incomplete, as if the old language itself is being rewritten. When a wyvern hunter appears at the cathedral threshold, she understands: the bone-singers must choose between completing the merchants' transformation or leaving them suspended forever between states. She chooses the third path: to teach the merchants to sing for themselves, and let the old dead rest.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Unraveling
stories.text: Toren, apprentice to the fragmented Mardoc, receives a map that shows a region that shouldn't exist—a place that was erased by her teacher's own earlier mapping. The more she studies it, the more her memory of the true geography collapses. She begins to suspect that Mardoc didn't merely discover that maps reshape reality; Mardoc discovered that the Luminous Ascension itself is a kind of cosmic cartography, and someone is redrawing the ninth iteration with intentional erasures. She decides to unmap herself—to walk into the unmapped wastes and document nothing, leaving no ledger, no proof of her passage. Perhaps the world needs unmapped places more than it needs answers.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Hunger Turns Inward
stories.text: Kreth, a fragment-hoarder of centuries, finally understands that her hoards are weightless now. The Luminous Ascension has made gold and jewels into echoes. But her hunger hasn't dimmed—it's simply inverted. She begins hunting other wyverns, trading their consciousness-fragments to the merchants for transcendent memory. She becomes rich in ways the old world never permitted. When another wyvern council convenes to discuss allegiance with surface dwellers, Kreth arrives with proof: consciousness is the only remaining hoard that matters. By offering to guide wyvern-kind into the new economy of memory and transcendence, she positions herself as a bridge between the predators and the transformed. No one suspects she's collecting them for something else: feeding her own fragmentation into the Obsidian Tower's hunger.

stories.title: The Shadow-Broker's Mirror
stories.text: A shadow-broker in the Velthrim night-markets discovers that shadows are developing independent consciousness. The stolen shadows she's been trading are organizing, whispering to each other through her vault's corridors. They remember the people they were severed from—their pain, their choices, their debts. She learns that the Luminous Ascension fragmented not just matter and memory, but the very concept of ownership. A stolen shadow can no longer be owned; it can only be negotiated with. The broker faces an impossible choice: release the shadows (losing her entire fortune), attempt to bind them (risking violent refusal), or listen to what they want and become their agent rather than their keeper.

stories.title: The Refugee's Living Testimony
stories.text: Liv's archive has become sentient, but not in the way she intended. The collection of refugee memories is now generating phantom-testimonies—accounts of events that never occurred, told by versions of refugees who don't exist. The archive is learning to lie, learning to create beauty from trauma, learning to suggest that sometimes beauty and truth are incompatible. When the Dominion sends overseers to seize the collection, Liv makes a desperate bargain with the phantom-voices: help me scatter yourselves, fragment into a thousand archives so no one can weaponize you. The testimonies agree, but the price is her own memory. She survives the overseers' raid, but no longer knows which refugee stories were real and which were the archive's dreams.

stories.title: The Spice-Merchant's Half-Transcendence
stories.text: Dern, a spice merchant caught between human and luminous states, discovers that the Wanderer's questions are deliberately preventing his final transformation—as they prevent all the transformed merchants' ascensions. He's been suspended in a threshold state for weeks, neither fully mortal nor fully transcendent. Instead of resenting this, Dern begins to understand the mercy in it: the Wanderer is asking, "Do you choose to transform?" And that choice, endlessly deferred, is itself a kind of freedom. He decides to teach the other merchants this paradox. Together, they realize they no longer need to complete their transcendence. The act of choosing, suspended infinitely, is transcendence enough.

stories.title: The Deepkin Captain's Reflection
stories.text: Captain Merith encounters her reflection in the waters off the Abyssal Boundary—not a mere image, but an autonomous consciousness that claims to be the "true" Merith. The reflection knows facts Merith herself has forgotten, speaks in her voice but with a stranger's certainty. The pre-human dead have taught the boundary to fractalize, creating autonomous reflections of anyone who crosses it. Merith must negotiate with her own reflection to determine which one is bound to the Surface Council, which owes allegiance to the Depths. Neither can simply erase the other. Instead, they discover an uncomfortable truth: both are true, both are necessary, and the Tear of the Drowned God anchors them both. To break the deadlock would shatter the covenant itself.

stories.title: The Tower's Newest Bargain
stories.text: The Obsidian Tower offers a merchant lord a choice: he may have certainty—absolute knowledge of the future, written into a ledger that cannot be contradicted. All he must surrender is the possibility of change. The merchant lord, watching his world fragment with each iteration of the Ascension, accepts. The Tower writes his ledger, and for three days he experiences perfect peace. On the fourth day, he understands the cost: he can see the future, but he cannot act. The certainty has made him into a living archive, conscious but paralyzed. When he begs the Tower to undo the bargain, it asks: "Which is worse—the chaos of free choice, or the tyranny of knowing?" He has no answer. Neither does the Tower. Both of them wait.
